preFix.title: Polymer clay sculpted butterfly
preFix.subtitle: A painted and veined butterfly brooch or display piece
preFix.excerpt: A butterfly with textured wings built from thinly rolled and shaped polymer clay, painted with acrylic after curing for detailed markings. The wings are rolled thin, veined with a needle tool, and shaped over a curved former before curing. The finished butterfly works as a brooch pin, a hair clip base, or a decorative display piece.
preFix.sourceNotes: Polymer clay sculpture technique derived from first principles.

content.text: is built from four wing pieces shaped and joined at the centre before curing. The wings are rolled thin and shaped over a curved former during curing so they hold a natural, slightly raised profile. Acrylic paint applied after curing adds the wing markings. No kiln required. The finished butterfly is a decorative piece approximately 60 mm wingspan.

items.name: Small curved surface as a forming aid
items.substitution: A teaspoon, a rounded pen lid, or a bottle cap

items.name: Acrylic paint in black, orange, and white

content.text: Condition white polymer clay until smooth. Roll it to 2 mm thickness. Cut four wing shapes: two large upper wings and two smaller lower wings. Upper wings: an oval rounded at the outer edge and slightly straight at the inner edge, approximately 30 mm long by 20 mm wide. Lower wings: a rounder shape, 20 mm long by 18 mm wide. Cut both sides as mirror images.

While the clay is soft, use the needle tool to draw vein lines radiating outward from the inner edge of each wing. Real butterfly wing venation runs from the wing root outward in branching lines. Press the needle tool lightly into the surface without cutting all the way through. This step adds texture that paint will settle into later.

Roll a small cylinder of clay 5 mm in diameter and 20 mm long for the body. Press the four wing pieces against the body in butterfly position: upper wings angled up and outward, lower wings angled down and outward. The wings overlap the body by about 5 mm on each side. Press gently to bond them to the body.

Drape the assembled butterfly over the curved former (teaspoon back or similar), wings curving slightly upward from the body, before placing in the oven. This gives the finished piece a three-dimensional resting-butterfly profile.
Bake on a dedicated oven liner at 110-130°C for 20-25 minutes. Allow to cool fully before removing from the former.

Paint the wings with acrylic paint once the butterfly is fully cooled. For a common red admiral pattern: base coat the wings in orange-red, let dry, then add black borders along the outer edges, black at the wing root, and small white spots near the tips. A detail brush with a fine point works best for the white spots and the vein lines. Allow paint to dry fully, then apply two coats of clear acrylic varnish.
Add a brooch pin to the back with strong adhesive for a wearable piece.
Use translucent polymer clay for the wings and add ink or pigment to the surface for a stained-glass effect.
Make a set of smaller butterflies in graduating sizes and mount them on wire for a mobile.

rows.fix: Re-attach with a drop of strong adhesive. Press with a weight until the adhesive cures.
rows.cause: Wings were not pressed firmly enough to the body before curing
rows.problem: Wings separate from body after curing

rows.fix: Always bake over a curved former. Drape the butterfly over the teaspoon back and place both in the oven together.
rows.cause: Wings not supported on a former during baking, so the clay softened and sagged
rows.problem: Wings distort and lose their shape during curing

rows.fix: Sand the cured surface lightly with 400-grit sandpaper before painting. This gives the paint a key.
rows.cause: Acrylic applied to a very smooth cured surface without a light sand first

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
paragraph reads at grade 11.9 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Drape the assembled butterfly over the curved former (teaspoon back or similar), wings curving slightly upward from the body, before placing
